source_label: BOLT-7: P2P Node and Channel Discovery
excerpt: channel_announcement requires signatures from both endpoint nodes and from both keys involved in the funding output. This anchors the announcement to a specific on-chain UTXO that any peer can verify. Without this proof, the network would be vulnerable to nodes flooding the graph with fictitious channels to manipulate routing.
body:
Source excerpt:
channel_announcement requires signatures from both endpoint nodes and from both keys involved in the funding output. This anchors the announcement to a specific on-chain UTXO that any peer can verify. Without this proof, the network would be vulnerable to nodes flooding the graph with fictitious channels to manipulate routing.

---

Cited by leaf "BOLT-7 gossip and the channel graph":
For routing to work, every Lightning node needs a reasonably fresh view of the public channel graph. BOLT-7 specifies the *gossip protocol* that propagates this information without requiring a central directory. Three message types do the work: `channel_announcement` advertises a new public channel (signed by both endpoint nodes and both funding-output keys, which proves the channel actually exists on the Bitcoin chain), `channel_update` advertises the current routing policy (fee rates, minimum and maximum HTLC sizes, `cltv_expiry_delta`), and `node_announcement` carries node metadata like the alias, colour, and the addresses on which the node accepts connections [1].

Channel announcements are anchored to the on-chain funding transaction. The announcing nodes provide the `short_channel_id` (block height, transaction index, output index), and any peer can verify that the named UTXO is in fact a 2-of-2 multisig of the announced keys. This is what stops a node from announcing fake channels — every claimed channel is backed by real, verifiable on-chain capital, and adding a fake announcement requires committing real bitcoin to a real funding transaction. When the funding output is later spent (channel close), the channel is implicitly removed from the graph by every node that observes the chain [2].

`channel_update` is the part that changes most often. A node typically re-broadcasts updates when it changes its fee policy or when its peer goes offline (signaling that the channel is currently unroutable in that direction). To prevent gossip spam, BOLT-7 enforces rate limits, stale-update suppression, and the requirement that updates be strictly newer than the last one accepted for the same channel/direction. Initial sync uses range queries (`query_channel_range` / `query_short_channel_ids`) so a fresh node can backfill the graph efficiently from one or two peers instead of waiting for the full broadcast cycle.

There is a fundamental tension in the design. More gossip means better path-finding and higher routing reliability, but it also leaks information about the network's topology and (indirectly) about which channels are seeing high traffic. *Private channels* — channels that are never announced via gossip — are routed to via *route hints* embedded in invoices: the receiver tells the sender, in the invoice, the specific channels that lead to them, signed but not broadcast publicly. Routing nodes that operate at scale typically have a mix of announced public channels (for inbound forwarding fees) and private channels (for direct settlement with major counterparties).

The gossip protocol is what makes the network discoverable but is also the part most under iteration. BOLT-7 v2 work and the proposed taproot-aware `channel_announcement_2` aim to reduce both the on-chain footprint per channel (by eliminating the need to publish both funding-output keys) and the bandwidth per gossip message. These changes also open the door to channels backed by something other than a 2-of-2 multisig — a meaningful simplification that has been blocked for years on coordination across implementations.
